Unlike conventional large-scale nuclear plants, SMRs are designed to be smaller, modular, and inherently safer, according to the background paper.

Each unit can be factory-built and transported for assembly, reducing construction risks and costs.

They also incorporate “passive safety” features by using natural forces like gravity and air circulation to cool the reactor without human intervention, thus making them less prone to accidents.

Globally, momentum is building for SMRs, with projects underway in countries such as the United States, Canada and China.

In Southeast Asia, Indonesia and Vietnam have outlined plans to develop nuclear power plants by 2030, while Malaysia and Thailand have also signalled interest in exploring new nuclear technologies.

These newer nuclear reactor designs are increasingly seen as a viable complement to renewable energy in achieving net-zero emissions.

Biomethane as a low-carbon fuel

Alongside nuclear, Singapore is also moving to pilot biomethane imports as a low-carbon fuel to decarbonise its natural gas-dependent power sector, which currently accounts for 95 per cent of Singapore’s electricity generation.

Biomethane, a renewable form of natural gas made from organic waste, can be fed directly into existing pipelines and used in current power plants without costly retrofits.

Under a new regulatory sandbox to be set up on Jurong Island, selected power generation companies will be able to test biomethane’s commercial and operational viability in a controlled environment, paving the way for wider market adoption.

Tan said such initiatives reflect Singapore’s “multi-pathway” approach to energy transition.

“We must pursue a diversified portfolio of sustainable energy pathways to build the future we envision. It is unlikely that any single solution will suffice, just as there is no one-size-fits-all solution to decarbonisation.”

The government’s latest announcements follow a series of clean energy projects on Jurong Island by Sembcorp Industries, including the Sembcorp Jurong Island Solar Farm, Singapore’s largest ground-mounted solar project to date, and an expanded vertical battery stacking solution to support grid stability.

These developments underline Singapore’s broader strategy to balance the so-called energy "trilemma" — sustainability, security, and affordability — as it works towards net-zero emissions by 2050.

Top images via "Building Singapore's Capabilities to Assess Nuclear Energy" background paper, Pixabay
actual_output: At SIEW, Tan See Leng announced Singapore is assessing nuclear energy via SMRs with passive safety, supported by EMA and NEA collaborations with the United States' Battelle Memorial Institute and Idaho National Laboratory. EMA engineer Shini Foo defends the misunderstood technology, vital because solar meets only 10 per cent of demand. Alongside a Jurong Island biomethane pilot utilizing existing pipelines, these innovations address the energy trilemma. This multi-pathway strategy targets net-zero emissions by 2050, mirroring broader Southeast Asian nuclear interest.

input:
Singapore officially launched the Communicable Diseases Agency (CDA), a new statutory board under the Ministry of Health (MOH), on Nov. 12 to protect the nation from infectious diseases and bolster its pandemic preparedness efforts.

The agency will lead in areas like research, updating detection strategies, international collaboration and public education.

The launch was graced by Prime Minister Lawrence Wong and Minister for Health and Coordinating Minister for Social Policy Ong Ye Kung.

Agency will lead pandemic preparedness efforts

In a Nov. 12 press release, CDA said that it consolidates public health and scientific expertise, operational and policy capabilities, surveillance, and analytics skills and systems.

Previously, these functions resided across MOH, the National Centre for Infectious Diseases (NCID) and the Health Promotion Board.

CDA has been operational since Apr. 1 this year and has already launched several initiatives, such as the Singapore Pandemic Preparedness and Response Framework .

CDA said that its strategy is built on three pillars: prepare, prevent and protect.

"This integrated approach enables CDA to tackle current and future infectious disease threats," the agency said.

In practice, CDA will be tasked with operations such as running research efforts on pandemic preparedness and response, updating surveillance strategies, and reviewing and enhancing the nation's immunisation framework.

The agency will also work with the healthcare institutions to strengthen infection prevention and control by developing national guidelines and standards.

In addition, CDA will roll out public education efforts and help keep healthcare professionals equipped with up-to-date knowledge and skills.

It will also work to boost early detection of disease threats and leverage new technologies to better respond to them.

Outside of Singapore, CDA will work with international partners to complement its national efforts.

To date, it has signed Memoranda of Understanding with counterparts in the People's Republic of China, Germany, Hong Kong, the Republic of Korea, and the United Kingdom.

"As infectious diseases emerge with increasing frequency due to globalisation, high population density, climate change, and evolving pathogens, the launch of CDA represents Singapore’s proactive approach to building our national resilience against infectious diseases," the agency said.

With the CDA launched, the NCID will focus on clinical care and outbreak preparedness.

Overcoming pandemic requires working together: PM Wong

Speaking at the launch, PM Wong, who is also the Finance Minister, shared that Covid-19 was the "crisis of our generation" and the next pandemic may come "sooner than we expect".

CDA was borne out of Singapore's experience from Covid-19, he said.

According to PM Wong, the agency and its partners will conduct regular exercises and simulations to "keep our systems warm".

"CDA has an ambitious and important mission. I am confident it has the right people to carry it out," the prime minister said.

PM Wong also called on Singaporeans to stay vigilant.

"Even so, the CDA cannot fight the next pandemic alone. Covid showed us that pandemics are not just public health issues – they can significantly impact our economy, our society, and the daily lives of every Singaporean. It took every sector of society to fight Covid. [...] When the next crisis comes, the Government cannot act alone. We will once again need the full support and participation of every Singaporean — to stay vigilant, to act responsibly and to stand together. Only by working together — as one united Singapore — can we overcome the next pandemic."

Top image via Mothership

The agency has already established the Singapore Pandemic Preparedness and Response Framework and signed agreements with counterparts in five countries.     *   *Source Text:* "CDA said that its strategy is built on three pillars: prepare, prevent and protect."
    *   *Verification:* Consistent.

5.  **Answer:** **Yes.**
    *   *Source Text:* "...has already launched several initiatives, such as the Singapore Pandemic Preparedness and Response Framework."

6.  **Answer:** **Yes.**
    *   *Source Text:* "To date, it has signed Memoranda of Understanding with counterparts in the People's Republic of China, Germany, Hong Kong, the Republic of Korea, and the United Kingdom." (Count: 5)
    *   *Verification:* Consistent.

7.  **Answer:** **Yes.**
    *   *Source Text:* "...the next pandemic may come 'sooner than we expect'..." and "We will once again need the full support and participation of every Singaporean — to stay vigilant, to act responsibly and to stand together."
    *   *Verification:* Consistent.

--- STAGE 4: CORRECTED FINAL SUMMARY ---
No factual errors were identified in the draft.

Final Summary:

Operational since April 1, the CDA consolidates expertise from various health bodies to lead research, surveillance, and international collaboration under a "prepare, prevent and protect" strategy. The agency has already established the Singapore Pandemic Preparedness and Response Framework and signed agreements with counterparts in five countries. Emphasizing that the next pandemic may arrive sooner than expected, PM Wong noted that the CDA's efforts must be complemented by public vigilance and unity.

--- STAGE 1: PROMPT DESIGN ---

**Analysis:**
*   **Article Type:** This is a hard news report regarding government policy and public health infrastructure. It is informative and factual, focusing on structural changes in Singapore's healthcare system.
*   **Source Style:** *Mothership* typically uses accessible, plain English to explain government announcements to the general public. The summary should mirror this clarity without oversimplifying the technical mandate of the new agency.
*   **Key Elements:** The summary must identify the new entity (CDA), its parent ministry (MOH), its core purpose (pandemic preparedness/consolidation of functions), its three-pronged strategy ("prepare, prevent, protect"), and the Prime Minister's message regarding national unity.
*   **Pitfalls:** A common pitfall here would be listing every specific function (e.g., listing all MOU countries) which would bloat the summary. Another risk is confusing the new role of the NCID with the role of the new CDA.

**Designed Prompt:**
"Generate a concise news summary of the provided text regarding the launch of Singapore's Communicable Diseases Agency (CDA).

Your summary must:
1.  **Identify** the CDA, its launch date, and its status as a statutory board under the Ministry of Health.
2.  **Explain** the agency's primary mandate (consolidating expertise) and its core strategy ('prepare, prevent, protect').
3.  **Clarify** the shift in responsibilities, specifically noting how CDA absorbs functions from other bodies while NCID shifts focus to clinical care.
4.  **Conclude** with Prime Minister Lawrence Wong's key message regarding the necessity of public cooperation in facing future pandemics.

**Constraints:** The output must be exactly 3-5 sentences long. Do not include outside information. Maintain a professional, objective tone."

--- STAGE 2: EXECUTE ---

**Final Summary:**
Singapore officially launched the Communicable Diseases Agency (CDA) on November 12, a new statutory board under the Ministry of Health designed to consolidate public health expertise and bolster national pandemic preparedness. Guided by a "prepare, prevent, and protect" strategy, the agency takes over surveillance, research, and outreach functions previously shared across multiple health bodies, while the National Centre for Infectious Diseases (NCID) pivots to focus on clinical care. The CDA has also engaged in international collaboration, signing agreements with partners in five countries to complement its national resilience efforts. At the launch, Prime Minister Lawrence Wong emphasized that the agency was born from the lessons of Covid-19, warning that the government cannot fight future pandemics alone and requires the united support of all Singaporeans.

Here is the step-by-step reasoning and final synthesis for the news summary.

--- STEP 1: GENERATE THREE CANDIDATE SUMMARIES ---

**Candidate A (Event-focused)**
Singapore officially launched the Communicable Diseases Agency (CDA) on November 12 as a new statutory board under the Ministry of Health to centralize the nation's infectious disease defense. The agency consolidates expertise previously spread across the ministry, the National Centre for Infectious Diseases (NCID), and the Health Promotion Board. It has been operational since April 1, focusing on tasks such as updating surveillance strategies and signing international Memoranda of Understanding. The launch was officiated by Prime Minister Lawrence Wong, who highlighted the agency's role in maintaining system readiness through regular exercises.

**Candidate B (Impact-focused)**
To strengthen national resilience against future epidemics, Singapore has established the Communicable Diseases Agency (CDA) to oversee the "prepare, prevent, and protect" strategy. The agency will lead critical public health functions, including research, early threat detection, and the development of national infection control guidelines, while the NCID pivots to focus on clinical care. This integrated approach aims to tackle threats arising from globalization and climate change by combining policy capabilities with scientific expertise. Prime Minister Lawrence Wong emphasized that while the CDA bolsters preparedness, overcoming future crises ultimately requires public vigilance and unity.

**Candidate C (Stakeholder-focused)**
Prime Minister Lawrence Wong and Health Minister Ong Ye Kung officiated the launch of the Communicable Diseases Agency (CDA), a body born from Singapore's experience with the Covid-19 crisis. The CDA will take over specific public health and surveillance functions from the Ministry of Health and NCID, collaborating with international partners in countries like China and the UK. PM Wong stated that the agency faces an ambitious mission to "keep our systems warm" through simulations and updated immunization frameworks. He warned that the government cannot act alone, calling on Singaporeans to support the agency's efforts when the next pandemic strikes.

--- STEP 2: EVALUATE CANDIDATES ---

**Candidate A (Event-focused)**
*   **Score:** 4/5
*   **Justification:** High faithfulness and solid factual coverage, but slightly dry and misses the "why" regarding the medical strategy.

**Candidate B (Impact-focused)**
*   **Score:** 5/5
*   **Justification:** Best fit for the "Health and Medicine" topic; clearly explains the agency's strategic pillars, the shift in NCID's role, and the public health implications.

**Candidate C (Stakeholder-focused)**
*   **Score:** 3/5
*   **Justification:** Accurate, but focuses too heavily on the politicians and bureaucracy rather than the agency's operational health functions.

--- STEP 3: SYNTHESIZE ---

**Final Summary**
Singapore has officially launched the Communicable Diseases Agency (CDA), a new statutory board under the Ministry of Health designed to consolidate the nation's expertise in surveillance, research, and pandemic response. Born from the lessons of Covid-19, the agency operates on a "prepare, prevent, and protect" strategy to oversee public education, international collaboration, and infection control guidelines while the NCID focuses on clinical care. At the launch, Prime Minister Lawrence Wong warned that the next pandemic may arrive sooner than expected, emphasizing that the CDA’s integrated approach must be bolstered by public vigilance and social unity.

input:
Football fans in Singapore will be able to watch some FIFA World Cup matches for free in 2026.

Mediacorp announced it has secured the broadcast rights to the tournament and a wider slate of FIFA competitions through 2028.

Under the agreement, Mediacorp will carry all 104 matches and official ceremonies of the FIFA World Cup 2026 across its digital and free-to-air platforms, positioning the national media network as Singapore’s “Home of FIFA” for the coming years.

28 matches to be shown for free

In addition to its subscription offerings, Mediacorp said it will significantly expand free coverage of the tournament, increasing the number of matches available without charge from nine games in 2022 to 28 in 2026.

The free-to-air matches, which will be shown on Channel 5 and mewatch , include:

All matches and ceremonies will also be accessible to subscribers through mewatch and Mediacorp’s carriage partner platforms.

Viewers can also continue to access classic football content through FIFA+ , a free channel available on mewatch.

The channel offers round-the-clock programming, including full-length classic matches, curated highlights, documentaries and “best of” compilations.

Largest FIFA ever

The 2026 edition will be the largest FIFA World Cup ever staged, featuring 48 participating teams for the first time.

The tournament will be co-hosted by Canada, Mexico and the United States, running from Jun. 11 to Jul. 19, 2026, with an expanded format that increases the number of matches from 64 to 104 over 39 days.

Beyond the men’s World Cup, Mediacorp’s deal also covers all matches and ceremonies of the FIFA Women’s World Cup 2027, along with several other FIFA tournaments between 2026 and 2028.

These include:

Mediacorp said further information on carriage partners, subscription plans and pricing will be released at a later date.

Top images via Canva, Unsplash

Notably, Mediacorp will offer 28 matches for free on Channel 5 and mewatch, a significant increase from the nine free matches aired during the 2022 World Cup. The deal also encompasses rights to the FIFA Women’s World Cup 2027 and various youth tournaments, though subscription pricing details have not yet been released.

--- STAGE 3: ANSWER AND VERIFY ---

2.  **Answer:** Yes. The text states: "Mediacorp will carry all 104 matches... of the FIFA World Cup 2026" and mentions the format "increases the number of matches from 64 to 104."

The free-to-air matches, which will be shown on Channel 5 and mewatch , include:

All matches and ceremonies will also be accessible to subscribers through mewatch and Mediacorp’s carriage partner platforms.

Viewers can also continue to access classic football content through FIFA+ , a free channel available on mewatch.

The channel offers round-the-clock programming, including full-length classic matches, curated highlights, documentaries and “best of” compilations.

This expanded tournament will feature a record 48 teams and 104 matches, all of which will be carried across Mediacorp’s digital and free-to-air platforms. In addition to the men's tournament, Mediacorp will broadcast the FIFA Women’s World Cup 2027 and other FIFA youth competitions. The network stated that details regarding subscription pricing and specific partners will be released at a later date.

--- STEP 2: EVALUATE CANDIDATES ---

**Candidate A (Event-focused)**
*   **Score:** 4/5
*   **Justification:** Accurately details the scope of the tournament and the timeline, but lacks specific details on the "free" aspect which is crucial for the "Entertainment" topic.

**Candidate B (Impact-focused)**
*   **Score:** 5/5
*   **Justification:** Best addresses the user interest (free entertainment) and clearly quantifies the benefit (9 vs 28 matches) while maintaining high coherence.

**Candidate C (Stakeholder-focused)**
*   **Score:** 3/5
*   **Justification:** While faithful to the text, the focus on corporate posturing ("Home of FIFA") is less engaging for a general summary than the specific viewing benefits found in Candidate B.

--- STEP 3: SYNTHESIZE ---

**Final Summary**
Mediacorp has secured broadcast rights for FIFA competitions through 2028, positioning itself as Singapore’s "Home of FIFA" starting with the expanded 2026 World Cup. Under this agreement, the network will significantly increase free-to-air coverage, offering 28 matches—including the semi-finals and final—for free on Channel 5 and mewatch, up from nine games in 2022. While subscribers will have access to all 104 matches of the record-breaking 48-team tournament, the deal also encompasses the 2027 FIFA Women’s World Cup and various youth competitions.

input:
Many N-Level students in Singapore would have been filled with anticipation for the release of their results on Dec. 18.

For two Secondary 4 (NA) students at Singapore Boys' Home, the day was extra special as a milestone in their journey to break free from a mottled past, marked with truancy, fights, and brushes with the law.

Mothership spoke to the boys, Jackson and Xavier (not their real names), about their experiences studying for the N-Levels in the Home, what the results mean to them, as well as their aspirations for the future.

Jackson's story

Jackson, 17, was an Express student at a local secondary school who fell into bad company.

When he was 14, he argued with his mother, ran away from home, and played truant for the most part of the year.

Jackson would spend his nights hanging out with friends, playing basketball, and taking joyrides on Personal Mobility Devices (PMDs).

He'd only sleep during the day.

Meanwhile, his grades plummeted from A2s and B3s, to E8s and F9s.

In 2024, he was charged with affray and placed on probation.

While recovering from a breakup, Jackson breached his probation by going out for night cycling with his friends.

He was subsequently sent to Singapore Boys' Home on a court order.

This proved to be a wake-up call for him.

"I'm pretty sure that I could tell what were the good influences and the bad influences. But at that point it's just that I wanted to hang on to the bad influences," said Jackson.

Some challenges he faced in the Home included the restricted television timings, lack of access to mobile phones, and strict regimentation, including regular muster calls.

But Jackson took these in his stride, believing it's important to "take charge" of himself.

To make the most of his time, he would work out regularly, and set aside time consistently to study in preparation for retaking his N-Levels.

This paid off as Jackson scored 8 points for his N-Levels, a night-and-day improvement from his previous score of 22.

When asked how he feels about the results, Jackson remarked, "Damn happy".

He plans to study law at a local polytechnic, become a lawyer, and let his mother retire early.

Another welcome change for Jackson is that he got closer to his mother and sister while in the Home.

He shared that he values their support and encouragement as they "haven't missed a single one" of the scheduled family visits to see him at the Home.

"I believe that they will all be waiting for me outside," he said.

Xavier's story

Xavier, 16, has had attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) from a young age.

His ADHD made it difficult to control his impulses, concentrate properly, or even sit still in class.

This led to him getting scolded by teachers, kicked out of class, and becoming "best friends" with his school discipline master.

At the age of 14, Xavier started skipping school. He'd mix with bad company and became involved in illicit activities.

"Each week, I would get into at least one fight," he said.

Subsequently, Xavier was charged in a cheating case and in May 2024, entered Singapore Boys' Home under a court order.

It was not all smooth sailing. He initially still got into fights at the Home, but as time passed, he began to change for the better.

Xavier credits his parents as his primary motivation to ask for help.

They consistently turned up for the scheduled parental visits at the Home, brought him out for meals during home leave, and never gave up on him, he said.

"They were always there for me. I don't want to disappoint them again," he said.

With the help of a team at Boys' Home – comprising teachers, medical professionals, psychologists, and rehabilitative specialists — Xavier started taking medication for his ADHD and worked on controlling his impulses.

He also became more intentional about repairing his relationship with family, as his time with them felt more precious.

Now, about one and a half years later, Xavier is no longer on medication as he feels confident in managing his impulses.

The smaller class sizes at the Home also benefitted Xavier, who felt more comfortable to ask questions in class.

In contrast to his earlier years where he felt like studies were "not important", Xavier began putting in the hours to prepare for his N-Level exams in October.

He even asked for extra consultations with his teachers to improve.

With a score of 17 points in his N-Levels, Xavier is applying for the Direct-Entry-Scheme to Polytechnic Programme (DPP).

If successful, he will be admitted directly into a two-year Higher Nitec programme.

He hopes to study business in university, become an entrepreneur, and support his family.

When asked if he had a message to share with teens in similar situations, Xavier said, "There's a right way and a wrong way to achieve your dreams. So don't give up."

The other results

All 32 youths who took their GCE N-Levels with the MSF Youth Homes qualified for admission to ITE this year.

Among the Normal (Academic) students, seven qualified for the Direct-Entry-Scheme to Polytechnic (DPP), Secondary 5N(A), or Polytechnic Foundation Programme (PFP).

Many of the Normal (Technical) students also achieved distinctions.

Ow Kwok Leong, principal for education at the MSF Youth Homes, said each result is "a testimony to the determination, effort, and aspirations of our youths, and their desire to succeed".

“Each youth’s result reflects success on his or her own terms, achieved through their perseverance, with the steadfast support and guidance of their schools, families, and staff of MSF," said Ow.

"Today, we celebrate not just their N-Level results, but their journey of rehabilitation and growth.”

Top image from MSF

Ow Kwok Leong, principal for education at the MSF Youth Homes, said each result is "a testimony to the determination, effort, and aspirations of our youths, and their desire to succeed".

“Each youth’s result reflects success on his or her own terms, achieved through their perseverance, with the steadfast support and guidance of their schools, families, and staff of MSF," said Ow.

"Today, we celebrate not just their N-Level results, but their journey of rehabilitation and growth.”

Top image from MSF

input:
Many N-Level students in Singapore would have been filled with anticipation for the release of their results on Dec. 18.

For two Secondary 4 (NA) students at Singapore Boys' Home, the day was extra special as a milestone in their journey to break free from a mottled past, marked with truancy, fights, and brushes with the law.

Mothership spoke to the boys, Jackson and Xavier (not their real names), about their experiences studying for the N-Levels in the Home, what the results mean to them, as well as their aspirations for the future.

Jackson's story

Jackson, 17, was an Express student at a local secondary school who fell into bad company.

When he was 14, he argued with his mother, ran away from home, and played truant for the most part of the year.

Jackson would spend his nights hanging out with friends, playing basketball, and taking joyrides on Personal Mobility Devices (PMDs).

He'd only sleep during the day.

Meanwhile, his grades plummeted from A2s and B3s, to E8s and F9s.

In 2024, he was charged with affray and placed on probation.

While recovering from a breakup, Jackson breached his probation by going out for night cycling with his friends.

He was subsequently sent to Singapore Boys' Home on a court order.

This proved to be a wake-up call for him.

"I'm pretty sure that I could tell what were the good influences and the bad influences. But at that point it's just that I wanted to hang on to the bad influences," said Jackson.

Some challenges he faced in the Home included the restricted television timings, lack of access to mobile phones, and strict regimentation, including regular muster calls.

But Jackson took these in his stride, believing it's important to "take charge" of himself.

To make the most of his time, he would work out regularly, and set aside time consistently to study in preparation for retaking his N-Levels.

This paid off as Jackson scored 8 points for his N-Levels, a night-and-day improvement from his previous score of 22.

When asked how he feels about the results, Jackson remarked, "Damn happy".

He plans to study law at a local polytechnic, become a lawyer, and let his mother retire early.

Another welcome change for Jackson is that he got closer to his mother and sister while in the Home.

He shared that he values their support and encouragement as they "haven't missed a single one" of the scheduled family visits to see him at the Home.

"I believe that they will all be waiting for me outside," he said.

Xavier's story

Xavier, 16, has had attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) from a young age.

His ADHD made it difficult to control his impulses, concentrate properly, or even sit still in class.

This led to him getting scolded by teachers, kicked out of class, and becoming "best friends" with his school discipline master.

At the age of 14, Xavier started skipping school. He'd mix with bad company and became involved in illicit activities.

"Each week, I would get into at least one fight," he said.

Subsequently, Xavier was charged in a cheating case and in May 2024, entered Singapore Boys' Home under a court order.

It was not all smooth sailing. He initially still got into fights at the Home, but as time passed, he began to change for the better.

Xavier credits his parents as his primary motivation to ask for help.

They consistently turned up for the scheduled parental visits at the Home, brought him out for meals during home leave, and never gave up on him, he said.

"They were always there for me. I don't want to disappoint them again," he said.

With the help of a team at Boys' Home – comprising teachers, medical professionals, psychologists, and rehabilitative specialists — Xavier started taking medication for his ADHD and worked on controlling his impulses.

He also became more intentional about repairing his relationship with family, as his time with them felt more precious.

Now, about one and a half years later, Xavier is no longer on medication as he feels confident in managing his impulses.

The smaller class sizes at the Home also benefitted Xavier, who felt more comfortable to ask questions in class.

In contrast to his earlier years where he felt like studies were "not important", Xavier began putting in the hours to prepare for his N-Level exams in October.

He even asked for extra consultations with his teachers to improve.

With a score of 17 points in his N-Levels, Xavier is applying for the Direct-Entry-Scheme to Polytechnic Programme (DPP).

If successful, he will be admitted directly into a two-year Higher Nitec programme.

He hopes to study business in university, become an entrepreneur, and support his family.

When asked if he had a message to share with teens in similar situations, Xavier said, "There's a right way and a wrong way to achieve your dreams. So don't give up."

The other results

All 32 youths who took their GCE N-Levels with the MSF Youth Homes qualified for admission to ITE this year.

Among the Normal (Academic) students, seven qualified for the Direct-Entry-Scheme to Polytechnic (DPP), Secondary 5N(A), or Polytechnic Foundation Programme (PFP).

Many of the Normal (Technical) students also achieved distinctions.

Ow Kwok Leong, principal for education at the MSF Youth Homes, said each result is "a testimony to the determination, effort, and aspirations of our youths, and their desire to succeed".

“Each youth’s result reflects success on his or her own terms, achieved through their perseverance, with the steadfast support and guidance of their schools, families, and staff of MSF," said Ow.

"Today, we celebrate not just their N-Level results, but their journey of rehabilitation and growth.”

Top image from MSF
actual_output:
Final Summary:
On Dec. 18, Singapore Boys' Home Secondary 4 (NA) students Jackson and Xavier celebrated N-Level results after overcoming troubled pasts. Jackson, a probation-breacher, scored 8 points, aiming for law. Xavier, managing ADHD, scored 17 points, applying for the Direct-Entry-Scheme to Polytechnic Programme (DPP) to become an entrepreneur. All 32 MSF Youth Homes candidates qualified for ITE admission, a feat Principal Ow Kwok Leong termed a testimony to their determination, rehabilitation, and family support, marking a significant milestone in their growth journey.

input:
Local football coach Gavin Lee, 35, is set to become the Lions' permanent head coach, reported local media.

Lee, who brought the Singapore national team to a historic Asian Cup qualification on Nov. 18, was previously interim head coach.

He will be the Football Association of Singapore's (FAS) first permanent local coach since 2016.

Historic win

Lee was appointed interim head coach after former head coach Tsutomu Ogura departed in June this year.

The Nanyang Technological University (NTU) alumnus was previously head coach of the BG Tampines Rovers.

During the Asian Cup qualifier, he led the Lions to a 2-1 win against Hong Kong, sending Singapore to the Asian Cup on merit for the first time in its history.

This led to calls for him to be appointed permanent head coach, despite earlier rumours of the FAS courting another foreign talent.

Accepted offer

The interim head coach was offered the full-time role and has accepted the offer, CNA reported.

He is scheduled to sign the contract on Nov. 27.

An official announcement will follow the signing of the 18-month contract, according to The Straits Times .

"Nothing’s been signed yet, but to be even considered in the first place, it’s a privilege, something I’ve mentioned many times before.

And ultimately, just like when I first started (as interim coach), all I want is the best for the national team and to continue the progress that I think we’ve made in the last few windows," he said.

As part of his new role, he is expected to lead the Lions to the 2026 Asean Championship and the 2027 Asian Cup.

Top image from Mothership and FAS/Singapore
actual_output:
Here is the step-by-step fact-checking and summarization process.

--- STAGE 1: DRAFT SUMMARY ---
Gavin Lee, 35, has accepted an offer to become the permanent head coach of Singapore's national football team, the Lions. He will be the first permanent local coach appointed by the Football Association of Singapore (FAS) since 2016. Lee, who served as interim coach following Tsutomu Ogura's departure in June, recently led the team to a historic Asian Cup qualification with a 2-1 win over Hong Kong on November 18. He is expected to sign an 18-month contract on November 27 to lead the team through the 2026 Asean Championship and 2027 Asian Cup.

--- STAGE 2: EXTRACT CLAIMS AND GENERATE VERIFICATION QUESTIONS ---

1.  **Claim:** Gavin Lee is 35 years old and has accepted the offer to become the permanent head coach of the Lions.
    *   **Question:** Is Gavin Lee identified as 35? Does the article state he accepted the offer to be permanent head coach?
2.  **Claim:** He will be the first permanent local coach appointed by the FAS since 2016.
    *   **Question:** Does the article state he is the first permanent local coach since 2016? Is the FAS the appointing body?
3.  **Claim:** Lee served as interim coach following Tsutomu Ogura's departure in June.
    *   **Question:** Was Lee the interim coach? Did Ogura depart in June?
4.  **Claim:** Lee led the team to Asian Cup qualification with a 2-1 win over Hong Kong on November 18.
    *   **Question:** Did the team qualify for the Asian Cup? Was the score 2-1 against Hong Kong? Did this happen on Nov 18?
5.  **Claim:** He is expected to sign an 18-month contract on November 27.
    *   **Question:** Is the contract length 18 months? Is the signing date Nov 27?

--- STAGE 3: ANSWER AND VERIFY ---

1.  **Question:** Is Gavin Lee identified as 35? Does the article state he accepted the offer to be permanent head coach?
    *   **Source Text:** "Local football coach Gavin Lee, 35, is set to become the Lions' permanent head coach... The interim head coach was offered the full-time role and has accepted the offer, CNA reported."
    *   **Verification:** Consistent.

2.  **Question:** Does the article state he is the first permanent local coach since 2016? Is the FAS the appointing body?
    *   **Source Text:** "He will be the Football Association of Singapore's (FAS) first permanent local coach since 2016."
    *   **Verification:** Consistent.

3.  **Question:** Was Lee the interim coach? Did Ogura depart in June?
    *   **Source Text:** "Lee... was previously interim head coach... after former head coach Tsutomu Ogura departed in June this year."
    *   **Verification:** Consistent.

4.  **Question:** Did the team qualify for the Asian Cup? Was the score 2-1 against Hong Kong? Did this happen on Nov 18?
    *   **Source Text:** "Lee, who brought the Singapore national team to a historic Asian Cup qualification on Nov. 18... he led the Lions to a 2-1 win against Hong Kong..."
    *   **Verification:** Consistent.

5.  **Question:** Is the contract length 18 months? Is the signing date Nov 27?
    *   **Source Text:** "He is scheduled to sign the contract on Nov. 27. An official announcement will follow the signing of the 18-month contract..."
    *   **Verification:** Consistent.

--- STAGE 4: CORRECTED FINAL SUMMARY ---
(No factual errors were found in the draft. The final summary remains the same as the draft.)

Final Summary:
Local coach Gavin Lee, 35, has accepted an offer to become the permanent head coach of Singapore's national football team, becoming the first local appointee to the role since 2016. Lee, who previously served as interim coach following Tsutomu Ogura's departure in June, recently guided the Lions to a historic Asian Cup qualification with a 2-1 victory over Hong Kong on November 18. According to local media, he is scheduled to sign an 18-month contract on November 27. His mandate includes leading the team through the 2026 Asean Championship and the 2027 Asian Cup.

actual_output:
--- STAGE 1: PROMPT DESIGN ---

**Analysis:**
*   **Type of Story:** This is a **hard news/sports reporting** piece focusing on a key personnel appointment. It combines factual reporting (contract details) with contextual background (recent match results).
*   **Source Style:** Mothership typically uses accessible, concise language with a focus on local relevance. The summary should be direct and professional but emphasize the "local" significance (e.g., highlighting that he is a local coach and an NTU alumnus).
*   **Key Elements:**
    1.  **The Event:** Gavin Lee accepting the permanent head coach role.
    2.  **The Trigger:** The historic Asian Cup qualification win against Hong Kong.
    3.  **The Significance:** First permanent local coach since 2016.
    4.  **The Logistics:** 18-month contract, signing date, and future tournament targets.
*   **Pitfalls to Avoid:**
    *   *Tense errors:* Distinguishing between the acceptance of the offer (done) and the signing of the contract (upcoming).
    *   *Omission of Context:* Failing to explain *why* he was hired (the historic win) makes the news less impactful.
    *   *Hallucination:* Adding details about specific players or salary not mentioned in the text.

**Designed Prompt:**
"Act as a sports journalist covering Singapore football. Produce a summary of the provided text that focuses on the confirmation of Gavin Lee as the permanent head coach. Your summary must adhere to the following structure:
1.  State the primary news hook regarding Lee's appointment and its historical significance regarding his nationality.
2.  Explain the specific achievement during his interim period that led to this decision.
3.  Detail the contract terms (duration/signing date) and his future objectives.

Keep the length strictly between 3 to 5 sentences. Do not use information outside of the provided text."

--- STAGE 2: EXECUTE ---

**Final Summary:**
Local football coach Gavin Lee has accepted an offer to become the permanent head coach of the Singapore Lions, marking the first time a local has held the permanent role since 2016. The 35-year-old secured the position after a successful interim tenure, during which he led the team to a historic 2-1 victory over Hong Kong to qualify for the Asian Cup on merit. Lee is scheduled to sign an 18-month contract on November 27. In his new capacity, he is expected to guide the national team through the 2026 Asean Championship and the 2027 Asian Cup.

BUDAPEST, Feb 3 - Hungary's centre-right Tisza party leads Prime Minister Viktor Orban's governing Fidesz party before an April 12 election, and the far-right Our Homeland party is on course to enter parliament, according to an opinion poll published on Tuesday.

Although pro-government pollsters put Fidesz ahead, the 21 Institute poll published by news site 24.hu was the second this week to put Tisza ahead and show Our Homeland winning enough seats to enter parliament.

Nationalist Orban faces the biggest challenge to his rule since Fidesz swept to victory in 2010 although the outcome remains highly uncertain, with opinion polls showing that many voters are still undecided.

The 21 Institute poll, conducted between January 28 and February 2, put Tisza on 35% support among all voters, up from 34% in December. Fidesz was on 28% support, up from 26%.

The poll showed 53% of decided voters supported Tisza and 37% backed Fidesz, a similar level to December.

The poll showed that Our Homeland (Mi Hazank) would be the only other party to pass the 5% threshold required for entry into parliament.

A Publicus Institute poll published on Monday by Nepszava newspaper showed 48% of decided voters supporting Tisza and 40% backing Fidesz, with Our Homeland on more than 5% support. It also showed 27% of voters were undecided, after 31% in December.

Tisza is led by former government insider Peter Magyar who has said his party would curb corruption, unlock billions of euros in frozen European Union funds to boost the economy, and firmly anchor Hungary in the EU and NATO. REUTERS
actual_output:
Here is the step-by-step fact-checking and summarization process.

--- STAGE 1: DRAFT SUMMARY ---
According to a 21 Institute poll published Tuesday, Hungary's centre-right Tisza party leads Prime Minister Viktor Orban's governing Fidesz party 35% to 28% among all voters ahead of the April 12 election. While pro-government pollsters still show Fidesz ahead, this surge represents the most significant challenge to Orban's rule since 2010. The far-right Our Homeland party is projected to be the only other group to cross the 5% threshold required to enter parliament. Tisza leader Peter Magyar has campaigned on curbing corruption and unlocking frozen EU funds to boost the economy.

--- STAGE 2: EXTRACT CLAIMS AND GENERATE VERIFICATION QUESTIONS ---
1.  **Claim:** The poll was conducted by the 21 Institute, published Tuesday, and concerns an April 12 election.
    *   **Question:** Is the pollster identified as 21 Institute, was it published on Tuesday, and is the election date listed as April 12?
2.  **Claim:** Tisza leads Fidesz 35% to 28% among all voters.
    *   **Question:** Are the specific percentages 35% and 28% accurate for the "all voters" category?
3.  **Claim:** Pro-government pollsters show Fidesz ahead, but this is the biggest challenge to Orban since 2010.
    *   **Question:** Does the article mention conflicting pro-government polls and characterize the situation as the biggest challenge since 2010?
4.  **Claim:** The far-right Our Homeland party is projected to cross the 5% threshold.
    *   **Question:** Is Our Homeland identified as far-right and is the threshold for entry confirmed as 5%?
5.  **Claim:** Tisza leader Peter Magyar aims to curb corruption and unlock frozen EU funds.
    *   **Question:** Is Peter Magyar identified as the leader and are these his stated policy goals?

--- STAGE 3: ANSWER AND VERIFY ---
1.  **Question:** Is the pollster identified as 21 Institute, was it published on Tuesday, and is the election date listed as April 12?
    *   **Source Text:** "Hungary's centre-right Tisza party leads... before an April 12 election... the 21 Institute poll published by news site 24.hu was the second this week... published on Tuesday."
    *   **Verification:** Consistent.
2.  **Question:** Are the specific percentages 35% and 28% accurate for the "all voters" category?
    *   **Source Text:** "The 21 Institute poll... put Tisza on 35% support among all voters... Fidesz was on 28% support."
    *   **Verification:** Consistent.
3.  **Question:** Does the article mention conflicting pro-government polls and characterize the situation as the biggest challenge since 2010?
    *   **Source Text:** "Although pro-government pollsters put Fidesz ahead... Nationalist Orban faces the biggest challenge to his rule since Fidesz swept to victory in 2010."
    *   **Verification:** Consistent.
4.  **Question:** Is Our Homeland identified as far-right and is the threshold for entry confirmed as 5%?
    *   **Source Text:** "...the far-right Our Homeland party... The poll showed that Our Homeland (Mi Hazank) would be the only other party to pass the 5% threshold required for entry into parliament."
    *   **Verification:** Consistent.
5.  **Question:** Is Peter Magyar identified as the leader and are these his stated policy goals?
    *   **Source Text:** "Tisza is led by former government insider Peter Magyar who has said his party would curb corruption, unlock billions of euros in frozen European Union funds..."
    *   **Verification:** Consistent.

--- STAGE 4: CORRECTED FINAL SUMMARY ---
No factual errors were found in the draft.

Final Summary:
According to a 21 Institute poll published Tuesday, Hungary's centre-right Tisza party leads Prime Minister Viktor Orban's governing Fidesz party 35% to 28% among all voters ahead of the April 12 election. While pro-government pollsters still show Fidesz ahead, this surge represents the most significant challenge to Orban's rule since 2010. The far-right Our Homeland party is projected to be the only other group to cross the 5% threshold required to enter parliament. Tisza leader Peter Magyar has campaigned on curbing corruption and unlocking frozen EU funds to boost the economy.

BERLIN - NATO has started military planning for an Arctic Sentry mission, a spokesperson for the alliance’s military headquarters SHAPE said on Feb 3, against a backdrop of tensions between the US and European allies over Greenland.

Repeated remarks by US President Donald Trump that he wants to acquire Greenland, accusing European allies of failing to properly secure the large Arctic island against Russia or China, have sparked a dispute with Copenhagen over the Danish overseas territory and triggered strains with NATO.

“Planning is under way for a NATO enhanced vigilance activity, named Arctic Sentry,” Colonel Martin O’Donnell, spokesperson for the Supreme Headquarters Allied Powers Europe, said, confirming a report by German magazine Spiegel.

He declined to provide additional details as planning had only just begun.

After meeting Mr Trump in Davos in January, NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte said they had discussed how NATO allies could work collectively to ensure Arctic security, including not just Greenland but the seven NATO nations with land in the Arctic.

Danish Defence Minister Troels Lund Poulsen said it was very gratifying that military planning for the NATO mission was underway.

“It is crucial that we work together with our NATO allies to increase security in the Arctic and the North Atlantic,” he said on social media platform X.

It was not immediately clear whether NATO defence ministers will discuss the topic at their Feb 12 meeting in Brussels.

Under the alliance’s rules, the NATO Supreme Allied Commander Europe, US General Alexus Grynkewich, has the authority to plan and execute “enhanced vigilance activities” without needing unanimous approval by the allies. AFP

Following a January meeting with Trump in Davos, NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte emphasized the need for collective security among the seven NATO nations with Arctic territory. Danish Defence Minister Troels Lund Poulsen welcomed the initiative, which is classified as an "enhanced vigilance activity" that US General Alexus Grynkewich can execute without unanimous alliance approval.

NEW YORK - A new investment thesis has spread through global markets at the start of 2026, as trading strategies long built on the primacy of the United States now opt for a new approach: Sell America.

The sentiment started to take hold in financial circles after the shock of sky-high tariffs sent stocks and bonds into a tailspin last April, but it has taken off recently as the Trump administration has pursued policies like attacks on the US Federal Reserve’s independence and threats of a new trade war with Europe that are worrying investors anew.
actual_output:
Here is the completed summarization and verification process:

--- STAGE 1: DRAFT SUMMARY ---
At the start of 2026, global markets are shifting toward a "Sell America" investment thesis, moving away from long-standing strategies focused on US primacy. This sentiment originated after sky-high tariffs caused stocks and bonds to crash last April. The trend has accelerated recently due to the Trump administration's attacks on the Federal Reserve’s independence and threats of a new trade war with Europe.

--- STAGE 2: EXTRACT CLAIMS AND GENERATE VERIFICATION QUESTIONS ---

1.  **Claim:** The shift in investment strategy is occurring at the start of 2026.
    *   **Question:** Does the article specify the start of 2026 as the time the thesis spread?
2.  **Claim:** The new strategy is called "Sell America" and replaces strategies based on US primacy.
    *   **Question:** Does the article identify the new approach as "Sell America" and the old approach as being built on US primacy?
3.  **Claim:** The sentiment started after sky-high tariffs caused a market tailspin "last April."
    *   **Question:** Does the text attribute the start of the sentiment to tariffs causing a tailspin in "last April"?
4.  **Claim:** The trend has intensified recently due to the Trump administration's policies.
    *   **Question:** Does the article attribute the recent takeoff of this sentiment to the Trump administration?
5.  **Claim:** Specific worrying policies include attacks on the Federal Reserve’s independence and threats of a trade war with Europe.
    *   **Question:** Does the article list attacks on the Fed and trade war threats against Europe as the specific causes for renewed worry?

--- STAGE 3: ANSWER AND VERIFY ---

1.  **Answer:** Yes. The article states: "A new investment thesis has spread through global markets at the start of 2026."
    *   **Status:** Consistent.
2.  **Answer:** Yes. The article states strategies were "long built on the primacy of the United States" and investors now "opt for a new approach: Sell America."
    *   **Status:** Consistent.
3.  **Answer:** Yes. The article states the sentiment "started to take hold... after the shock of sky-high tariffs sent stocks and bonds into a tailspin last April."
    *   **Status:** Consistent.
4.  **Answer:** Yes. The article states it "has taken off recently as the Trump administration has pursued policies..."
    *   **Status:** Consistent.
5.  **Answer:** Yes. The article cites "policies like attacks on the US Federal Reserve’s independence and threats of a new trade war with Europe."
    *   **Status:** Consistent.

--- STAGE 4: CORRECTED FINAL SUMMARY ---

Final Summary:
At the start of 2026, global markets are adopting a "Sell America" investment thesis, moving away from strategies built on United States primacy. This sentiment originated after high tariffs caused a decline in stocks and bonds last April. It has recently intensified due to the Trump administration's attacks on the Federal Reserve’s independence and threats of a new trade war with Europe.

input:
SINGAPORE – Scientists here will soon launch a small, lightweight “nanosatellite” carrying AI-powered edge computing, allowing it to analyse and prioritise information to be sent back to Earth, such as images unobscured by cloud cover.

The satellite will be tested during a one-year mission – the first of its kind for Singapore, led by the Satellite Research Centre (SaRC) at Nanyang Technological University (NTU) – to evaluate its electronics performance and overall operational design.

Its artificial intelligence (AI) can be directed to prioritise cloud-free images or flag urgent events such as forest fires and oil spills, with only useful data relayed back to Earth.

Compact outputs sent by the AI-powered satellite – such as key coordinates, image analysis summaries and selected image snippets – may comprise just kilobytes or a few megabytes of data, compared with raw satellite imagery that can range in size from hundreds of megabytes to gigabytes.

This significantly cuts transmission time.

This could be useful in responding to time-sensitive scenarios, such as oil spills or earthquake recovery efforts in the future.

The AI project will be integrated into a 30cm by 10cm by 10cm satellite built by space technology firm Satoro Space, a Singapore-based “satellite-as-a-service” solutions company.

The sub-5kg unit will be launched into orbit at around 500km above the Earth in 2026.

The AI-powered satellite was one of three new projects announced on Feb 2 by NTU, and supported by the national Space Technology Development Programme (STDP).

The Government has earmarked over $200 million since 2022 for STDP to boost research and development and spur the growth of Singapore’s space technology ecosystem.

Satellites can communicate and transmit data only when they pass over a ground station on Earth. At most, this contact window is about 10 minutes each time. Sun-synchronous orbits, which most Singapore satellites use, make a pass every 100 minutes.

SaRC executive director Lim Wee Seng said raw satellite imaging data accumulates quickly, and a small satellite may collect up to gigabytes of imagery over a day.

“Instead of sending everything back to Earth, the satellite can make decisions on board – filtering, analysing, and transmitting information, not just raw data. This dramatically reduces latency and makes space systems far more efficient.”

He added: “Intelligent satellites can decide what matters on board, ensuring precious space bandwidth is used to deliver answers, not noise.”

The mission will also test next-generation solar cells – mounted on the same satellite – in space. These perovskite solar cells are a new type of solar technology that is cheaper and easier to make than traditional solar panels, and can be printed onto flexible, lightweight materials.

Mr Lim said it will take “a few years” till the AI satellite’s cutting-edge technologies are commercialised.

Similar technologies to optimise information sent back to Earth using AI started around 2020, with the European Space Agency’s PhiSat-1 CubeSat demonstrating onboard machine-learning inference to filter cloudy imagery before sending the data to ground stations.

The second project NTU announced is a nanosatellite to be deployed in 2028 to test a new propulsion system that will allow better manoeuvring in space during a 1½-year mission.

The third is a space imaging camera, which uses three mirrors instead of lenses to deliver sharper images, that will be installed on the International Space Station in 2027 for six months.

*   **Key Elements:**
    *   **The Core Innovation:** A nanosatellite using AI edge computing to process data in orbit (filtering clouds, prioritizing urgent events) to save bandwidth.
    *   **Stakeholders:** NTU’s Satellite Research Centre (SaRC), Satoro Space, and the Singaporean Government (STDP funding).
    *   **Timeline:** Launch in 2026.
    *   **Secondary Context:** Inclusion of perovskite solar cells and mention of two other related space projects (propulsion test, ISS camera).
*   **Pitfalls to Avoid:**

    *   **Technical Overload:** Focusing too much on physical dimensions (30cm x 10cm) rather than the operational benefit (latency reduction).

"Act as a science and technology reporter. Summarize the provided article about Singapore's new satellite initiative. Your summary must focus on the primary subject: the AI-powered nanosatellite, its specific purpose regarding data transmission, and the entities involved. Briefly mention that this is part of a wider suite of projects announced by NTU.

***

input:
Feb 3 - Nigeria's state police on Tuesday charged ex-justice minister Abubakar Malami and his son, Abdulaziz, with five counts of terrorism‑related and firearms offences, months after the financial crimes watchdog charged them for money laundering.

Prosecutors allege Malami, in November 2022, knowingly abetted terrorism financing by refusing to prosecute suspected terrorism financiers whose case files were forwarded to him while he served as attorney general.

The pair pleaded not guilty and were ordered to remain in the custody of the Department of State Security ahead of commencement of trial on February 20 after rejecting an oral bail.

The arraignment adds to Malami's widening legal troubles, which include separate ongoing money‑laundering proceedings and a related asset‑forfeiture case before the Federal High Court.

In December, Malami was charged with 16 counts of money laundering and conspiracy involving billions of naira.

Defence lawyers argued the pair had already spent two weeks in detention and were brought to court from the hospital, but the judge insisted on a formal bail motion.

Prosecutors also claimed Malami and his son were found in December 2025 in possession of a firearm and cartridges without a valid licence.

These offences are punishable under provisions of Nigerian law. REUTERS

The defendants pleaded not guilty on Tuesday and were remanded to the custody of the Department of State Security until their trial begins on February 20. These new charges follow separate proceedings initiated in December where Malami was charged with 16 counts of money laundering.

Prosecutors allege Malami, in November 2022, knowingly abetted terrorism financing by refusing to prosecute suspected terrorism financiers whose case files were forwarded to him while he served as attorney general.

The pair pleaded not guilty and were ordered to remain in the custody of the Department of State Security ahead of commencement of trial on February 20 after rejecting an oral bail.

The arraignment adds to Malami's widening legal troubles, which include separate ongoing money‑laundering proceedings and a related asset‑forfeiture case before the Federal High Court.

In December, Malami was charged with 16 counts of money laundering and conspiracy involving billions of naira.

Defence lawyers argued the pair had already spent two weeks in detention and were brought to court from the hospital, but the judge insisted on a formal bail motion.

Prosecutors also claimed Malami and his son were found in December 2025 in possession of a firearm and cartridges without a valid licence.

These offences are punishable under provisions of Nigerian law. REUTERS

input:
GRAZALEMA, Spain – Storm Leonardo pounded the Iberian peninsula with torrential rains on Feb 5, prompting more flood warnings, as a man was killed by a deluge in Portugal and Spanish rescuers searched for a woman swept away by a river as she tried to save her dog.

Leonardo is the latest in a wave of half a dozen winter storms that have swept across Portugal and Spain since the start of 2026, killing several people, ripping roofs off homes and flooding towns.

Flooding is becoming more frequent across Europe as the atmosphere warms and holds more moisture due to climate change, scientists say. Unprecedented flash floods killed 237 people in Spain’s Valencia region in October 2024.

A man of around 70 died on Feb 4 in Portugal’s southern Alentejo region after his car was swept away on a flooded road near a dam, the Portuguese authorities said.

In southern Spain’s Malaga province, a girl went missing after she was dragged away by the Turvilla river while trying to rescue her dog.

“We spent the whole afternoon and night yesterday searching in the river from the place where the girl fell in until the very end of the river. We found the dog, but not her,” Malaga fire chief Manuel Marmolejo said on Spanish television.

Mountains can’t absorb any more water

In Grazalema, a village in Andalusia’s mountains popular with hikers, water seeped through the walls of houses and down the village’s steep cobbled streets.

“The mountains are full, they can’t absorb any more water. In some of the old houses, the old sewers are overflowing. The concern is that it won’t stop raining,” said restaurant owner Jose Maria Barea, 39.

President of Andalusia’s regional government Juan Manuel Moreno said Grazalema had received the same amount of rain in 16 hours as falls in the Madrid region in a year.

Two reservoirs down the mountain from Grazalema were at risk of overflowing. The authorities planned to drain them, he said. Storm Marta, the next weather front in the so-called “storm train”, is expected to hit the region over the weekend, according to state weather agency Aemet.

In southern Portugal, people waded waist-deep through Alcacer do Sal after the river Sado breached its banks. Restaurant terraces were completely underwater, with sandbags stacked in front of doors to protect homes and shops.

“I’ve never seen anything like this. It’s surreal,” said resident Maria Cadacha. “There are a lot of people here, very good people, many shopkeepers, homes with damage. I wouldn’t want to be in their shoes.” REUTERS
actual_output:
Here are the four stages of the summarization and verification process.

--- STAGE 1: DRAFT SUMMARY ---
Storm Leonardo struck Spain and Portugal on February 5 with torrential rains, marking the latest in a series of winter storms since the start of 2026. In Portugal, a 70-year-old man died when his car was swept away, while in Spain's Malaga province, a girl remains missing after entering the Turvilla river to save her dog. Andalusia's regional president Juan Manuel Moreno reported that the village of Grazalema received a year's worth of rain in just 16 hours. Authorities are preparing to drain reservoirs as another weather system, Storm Marta, is forecast to hit the region this weekend.

--- STAGE 2: EXTRACT CLAIMS AND GENERATE VERIFICATION QUESTIONS ---
1.  **Claim:** Storm Leonardo struck Spain and Portugal on February 5.
    *   *Question:* Does the article confirm the storm hit on Feb 5?
2.  **Claim:** It is the latest in a series of storms since the start of 2026.
    *   *Question:* Does the article state the timeframe of the storms is since the start of 2026?
3.  **Claim:** A 70-year-old man died in Portugal when his car was swept away.
    *   *Question:* Is the man’s age exactly 70, and did he die in the manner described?
4.  **Claim:** A girl is missing in Malaga after entering the Turvilla river to save a dog.
    *   *Question:* Is the victim identified as a girl, and did the event happen in the Turvilla river in Malaga?
5.  **Claim:** Juan Manuel Moreno reported that Grazalema received a year's worth of rain in 16 hours.
    *   *Question:* Did Moreno compare Grazalema's rainfall to its own yearly average, or a different benchmark?
6.  **Claim:** Authorities are draining reservoirs due to the upcoming Storm Marta.
    *   *Question:* Are reservoirs being drained, and is Storm Marta expected this weekend?

--- STAGE 3: ANSWER AND VERIFY ---
1.  **Question:** Does the article confirm the storm hit on Feb 5?
    *   *Source:* "Storm Leonardo pounded the Iberian peninsula with torrential rains on Feb 5..."
    *   *Verification:* **Consistent.**
2.  **Question:** Does the article state the timeframe of the storms is since the start of 2026?
    *   *Source:* "...wave of half a dozen winter storms that have swept across Portugal and Spain since the start of 2026..."
    *   *Verification:* **Consistent.**
3.  **Question:** Is the man’s age exactly 70, and did he die in the manner described?
    *   *Source:* "A man of around 70 died on Feb 4 in Portugal’s southern Alentejo region after his car was swept away..."
    *   *Verification:* **Inconsistent.** The draft says "70-year-old," but the text says "around 70." The draft implies the death happened during the Feb 5 storm, but the text specifies he died on Feb 4.
4.  **Question:** Is the victim identified as a girl, and did the event happen in the Turvilla river in Malaga?
    *   *Source:* "In southern Spain’s Malaga province, a girl went missing after she was dragged away by the Turvilla river..." (Note: The lead paragraph mentions a "woman," but the detailed body paragraph and quote refer to "girl").
    *   *Verification:* **Consistent** (aligned with the detailed body text).
5.  **Question:** Did Moreno compare Grazalema's rainfall to its own yearly average, or a different benchmark?
    *   *Source:* "...Grazalema had received the same amount of rain in 16 hours as falls in the Madrid region in a year."
    *   *Verification:* **Inconsistent.** The draft says "received a year's worth of rain," which implies Grazalema's own yearly average. The text specifically compares it to the *Madrid region's* yearly rainfall.
6.  **Question:** Are reservoirs being drained, and is Storm Marta expected this weekend?
    *   *Source:* "The authorities planned to drain them... Storm Marta... is expected to hit the region over the weekend..."
    *   *Verification:* **Consistent.**

--- STAGE 4: CORRECTED FINAL SUMMARY ---
Storm Leonardo pounded the Iberian Peninsula with torrential rains on February 5, continuing a wave of storms affecting the region since early 2026. In Portugal, a man around 70 years old died on February 4 after his car was swept away, while in Spain's Malaga province, rescuers are searching for a girl dragged away by the Turvilla river while trying to save her dog. Andalusia's regional president stated that the village of Grazalema received as much rain in 16 hours as the Madrid region typically gets in a year. Authorities plan to drain threatened reservoirs before the next system, Storm Marta, arrives over the weekend.

Final Summary:
Storm Leonardo pounded the Iberian Peninsula with torrential rains on February 5, continuing a wave of storms affecting the region since early 2026. In Portugal, a man around 70 years old died on February 4 after his car was swept away, while in Spain's Malaga province, rescuers are searching for a girl dragged away by the Turvilla river while trying to save her dog. Andalusia's regional president stated that the village of Grazalema received as much rain in 16 hours as the Madrid region typically gets in a year. Authorities plan to drain threatened reservoirs before the next system, Storm Marta, arrives over the weekend.

**Designed Prompt:**
"Generate a neutral, hard-news summary of the provided text for a general readership. The summary must consist of exactly 3 to 5 sentences.

***

Synopsis: Every first and third Tuesday of the month, The Straits Times analyses the beat of the changing environment, from biodiversity conservation to climate change.

Right now, it seems we’re in an ‘ice age’ for climate action. Geopolitics is all we can talk about and it’s pretty much wiped climate change and nature from the headlines. Wars and conflict, trade rows, attacks on climate policies and science and the fracturing of multilateralism have created a more fraught and uncertain world.

Some countries and businesses have weakened their environmental targets and policies, even as the climate and nature crises worsen. So what can be done? Can the shift to bilateral and regional climate and energy agreements replace unified global action? Can renewable energy remain a powerful uniting force? And how can we get climate and nature back into the headlines?

Join hosts Audrey Tan and David Fogarty as they discuss these questions and more in the latest episode of The Straits Times’ Green Pulse podcast.

Have a listen and let us know what you think!

Highlights of conversation (click/tap above):

1:51 For all the talk of fossil fuels, renewables are stealing the show

7:59 Rush for resources and the Greenland reality check

12:59 Weakening of multilateralism is changing the way we talk about climate and nature.

18:51 Focus on climate and nature might be in a rut. What will it take to refocus minds?

25:33 The human touch. Journalists need to put people at the centre of their environment stories.

**Analysis of Source:**
*   **Type:** Podcast Synopsis/Analysis. This is a promotional summary for an audio episode ("Green Pulse") that discusses trends in global climate politics. It is not a standard hard news report but rather a thematic overview of a discussion.
*   **Style:** The Straits Times generally employs a formal, objective, and concise British English style. However, as this is a podcast synopsis, the tone is slightly more conversational and inquisitive (posing questions to be answered). The summary should strip away the promotional language ("Join hosts...", "Have a listen...") and focus on the substantive arguments and topics presented.
*   **Key Elements:**
    *   The core thesis: Geopolitics and conflict are overshadowing climate action (the "ice age" metaphor).
    *   Specific sub-topics: The resilience of renewable energy, the decline of multilateralism, resource competition (Greenland), and the role of journalism.
    *   Format recognition: Acknowledging this is a discussion between hosts Audrey Tan and David Fogarty.
*   **Pitfalls:**
    *   **Hallucination:** Inventing the *conclusions* of the podcast. The text asks questions (e.g., "Can renewable energy remain a powerful uniting force?") but does not explicitly provide the hosts' final answers, only the topics. The summary must state what is *discussed*, not necessarily the final verdict unless explicitly stated in the highlights.
    *   **Clutter:** Including the extensive credits, social media links, or production details.

**Designed Prompt:**
"Summarize the provided podcast synopsis from The Straits Times' 'Green Pulse' series. Your summary must focus on the thematic tension between rising geopolitical conflict and the stagnation of global climate action. You must include the specific sub-topics mentioned in the conversation highlights, such as the status of renewable energy, the resource rush in Greenland, and the proposed strategy for journalists to re-engage audiences. Ensure the tone is objective and informative. Limit the output to exactly 3-5 sentences and base all statements strictly on the provided text."

In the latest episode of The Straits Times’ "Green Pulse" podcast, hosts Audrey Tan and David Fogarty discuss the current "ice age" for climate action, where geopolitical conflicts and trade disputes have displaced environmental concerns from the headlines. The conversation examines the fracturing of multilateralism and questions whether renewable energy can remain a unifying global force amidst a resource rush in places like Greenland. Furthermore, the episode addresses the role of media, suggesting that journalists must place people at the center of environmental stories to successfully refocus public attention on the climate crisis.

Despite the breakdown of multilateral cooperation, renewable energy remains a significant sector, though the global rush for resources faces reality checks in regions like Greenland. To combat this stagnation and return the climate crisis to the forefront, experts argue for a shift away from abstract policy talk toward narratives that put people at the center of environmental stories.

**Candidate C (Stakeholder-focused)**
Hosts Audrey Tan and David Fogarty analyze the fracturing of global environmental consensus, questioning if governments and businesses are retreating from their climate commitments. They debate the shifting landscape where renewable energy succeeds despite political friction and examine the behavior of nations scrambling for resources in the Arctic. The hosts conclude that for media and policy stakeholders to break through the current geopolitical noise, they must pivot to a "human touch" approach to make environmental issues resonate again.

In a recent episode of *The Straits Times*’ Green Pulse podcast, hosts Audrey Tan and David Fogarty analyze how geopolitical instability and conflict have created an "ice age" for global climate action. As multilateralism fractures and nations weaken environmental targets, the discussion explores whether renewable energy can remain a uniting force and if bilateral agreements can replace global consensus. The hosts also examine the resource rush in Greenland and argue that returning climate change to the headlines requires a shift toward more human-centric storytelling.

input:
Researchers at Carnegie Mellon University in the US are developing a simple screening test that is able to detect more than 30 types of Stage 1 cancers from the comfort of a patient’s home, in a move set to transform cancer treatment.

In an interview published in the university’s newsletter, the project’s lead investigator Rebecca Taylor said: “The elevator pitch for this project is: It would be wonderful to be able to screen for a range of small tumour types and do it effectively, fairly easily, at home every year.

“The challenge is that solid tumours at Stage 1 are super hard to detect. But if you can find them that early, they’re far easier to treat.”

At the heart of this ground-breaking project, which started in October and has secured US$26.7 million (S$34 million) worth of federal funding, is a multi-step screening process.

First, a person swallows a bioengineered pill packed with sensors that are triggered by key cancer indicators – low oxygen, acidity and elevated lactate.

These sensors release signals that indicate the presence of a tumour and its tissue of origin, and pass harmlessly out of the body in urine.

The patient takes a urine test, where a compact device is used to analyse whether there is a tumour and in which organ it is located, with results sent via smartphone to a medical professional for further analysis.

Professor Taylor said: “If we really want to make a dent in reducing the toll of cancer, this is a huge opportunity.

“What we’re attempting to do is understand how to find it. That’s very different from understanding the mechanisms of the disease, which is absolutely critical. But this project goes in a new direction.”

Project co-lead Burak Ozdoganlar said the project has the potential to ease healthcare costs and boost health outcomes.

“If we can make early detection something people can do at home, the impact could reach millions of people,” he said.

Early detection of solid tumours, particularly at Stage 1, is challenging but crucial, as it greatly boosts treatment success rates, said Prof Taylor.

The researchers plan to move the cancer-detection kit testing into human trials and sell it to patients at less than US$100.

This innovation is part of a US federal initiative aimed at creating over-the-counter multi-cancer early detection tests to increase the likelihood of effective treatment and survival.

Technical and social hurdles

For all the promise that such innovations offer, however, experts say there are significant technical and social hurdles to overcome.

Early detection tools must be both sensitive (able to detect tiny tumours) and specific (able to distinguish cancer from non-cancerous conditions).

False positives can lead to unnecessary anxiety and procedures; false negatives can provide false reassurance. New tools must also be rigorously validated in clinical trials.

Costs will be another critical factor.

The Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health, the federal agency funding the research, said projects should work towards a US$100 selling price – a steep target given the complexity of the technology.

“It’ll be a big challenge to pull that off, but we think we know how to make it possible too,” said Prof Taylor.

Her team is working with community partners like UPMC Hillman Cancer Center to find out what would be useful to potential users and what they can afford.

Carnegie Mellon’s project remains in its early stages with laboratory work under way, but within its process lies the promise of transforming cancer diagnosis – and the lives of many.

The process involves swallowing sensors that signal tumors through urine, which is analyzed by a compact device and smartphone to determine the cancer's location. 5.  **Question:** Is the price target under $100? Do experts mention sensitivity and false positives as hurdles?

--- STAGE 4: CORRECTED FINAL SUMMARY ---

The initiative, which began in October and secured US$26.7 million in federal funding from the Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health, utilizes sensors to identify tumor presence and organ location via a smartphone-connected device. The team aims to price the kit under US$100 to increase accessibility, though experts caution that the technology faces significant hurdles regarding validation, sensitivity, and the risk of false positives.
